title: Dog Refusing to Eat and Lying Still: Triage Guide for Pet Parents
meta_desc: Is your dog lethargic and not eating? Learn about the causes, urgency levels, and how to triage your pet's symptoms effectively.
content_html:
What is it?

When a dog refuses food (anorexia) and remains unusually still or listless (lethargy), it is a sign that their body is diverting energy to manage an underlying health issue. These are non-specific clinical signs, meaning they could be caused by a wide range of conditions including gastrointestinal upset, infection, metabolic disease, or internal pain.

Is it an emergency?

The urgency level for a dog that is both inappetent and lethargic is typically classified as Medium. If your dog has been acting this way for more than 12 to 24 hours, you should contact your veterinarian for a same-day appointment. It becomes an immediate emergency if you notice secondary symptoms such as pale gums, persistent vomiting, or a bloated, hard abdomen.

Why a photo helps triage

Taking a photo or a short video of your dog in their current state can be incredibly helpful for a veterinary triage team. A visual record allows professionals to assess your dog’s posture, respiratory rate, and facial expressions. For example, a video showing your dog struggling to get up or a photo of their gum color can help us determine if your pet needs to be seen immediately or if they can wait for a scheduled appointment.

faqs.question: How long can I wait if my dog isn't eating?
faqs.answer: If a healthy adult dog skips one meal but is otherwise acting normal, you can monitor them. However, if they are also lying still and acting lethargic, you should consult a vet within 24 hours.

faqs.question: Can I give my dog human medicine to help?
faqs.answer: No. Never give your dog human medications like Tylenol or Advil, as these are highly toxic to pets and can cause liver or kidney failure.

faqs.question: Should I try to force-feed my dog?
faqs.answer: It is generally not recommended to force-feed a lethargic dog, as this can lead to choking or aspiration pneumonia. It is better to wait for a veterinary assessment.
